Okay, so now you've gotten to the interview. Let's talk about the interview questions you're going to ask. So it is more important that you understand why you're asking these questions than it is that you ask those exact questions. So let me under explain to you. First one, why are you looking for a new position? And basically you want to know, you want to be looking for what the reason is, like were they fired, were they not passionate, maybe they're going to blame the company, you definitely don't want that, right? But a lot of times I like to ask somebody a question off that which is, okay, what is it that you're looking for in this job that you may not have had in the one that you just left or that you were just let go from, right? And so you want to understand their why. Two is what attracted you to our company enough to attend an interview, right? So this is basically getting them to sell themselves on your company as well as finding out what the real why is. Because if they're a true believer in your company, they'll say something to basically test wanting to be a part of your culture, they see that you're building something really cool, they see all the client testimonials, that's the kind of stuff you want to look for, right? What is your biggest weakness, right? This is something that people will tell you what their weaknesses. People are often more truthful than we like to think. The fact is that most people ignore it and they're like, oh, well, you don't seem that way, you're probably exaggerating. But when people tell you who they are, you need to listen, you need to know that they are being honest with you. And so don't take this question for granted. And think to yourself, okay, given that weakness, can we accommodate for it? How would we, is there somebody else on the team who has that as a strength? You want to start thinking like that, right? Do they fit in with this puzzle? And then tell me what your ideal job looks like, okay? So I like to say to people, all right, so tell me what your ideal job looks like. Say you come into gym launch tomorrow. What does it look like? Perfect world, right? And so pay close attention to if that ideal job aligns with what you're offering them. Is it the same hours? Does it sound like the same department? Does it sound like the same responsibilities that are of this job, right? Is it fast-paced, slow-paced? What's the environment that they're describing to you? Is it a reflection of your culture, right? What do you enjoy doing during your free time? Okay, so a lot of the times I like to gauge, is this person obsessed with working? Are they going to work all hours of the day? Do they have hobbies they like? Do they have a family? What is it that makes them who they are? And where do their values lie? What's important to them is creativity important, is getting outside important. What are the things they like to do? This is also just remember, getting to know someone, right? What kind of hours are you looking for? Again, you want to make sure that they're willing to work minimum hours that you need. And you need to express very clearly what hours are needed. And if they're not specific, if it's like, I need you to be available from this time to this time, generally, right? And so you need to make sure that you are aligning and they know exactly what the hours will be. Going hand in hand with that, you need to ask what is your minimum required income? Because you need to know that a lot of people will take a job for less than they actually need to get by because they hope or they expect to get a raise early on. Or they think, okay, I can get a job on the weekends and at nights because I'll be working this one. And so you need to ask them, what's your minimum required income so that you can work here and only here because you guys want people who are super dedicated. Okay. And so you also need to remember like people do not operate well, place of scarcity. And so if you're hiring someone on and they're going to be in a scarcity mindset, that's not going to work out very well.
